title: XXVIII
html:
XXVIII

In love!пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњsaid the corporalпїњвАФyour honour was very well the day before yesterday, when I was telling your honour the story of the King of BohemiaпїњвАФBohemia! said my uncle Toby -¬†-¬†-¬†- musing a long time -¬†-¬†- What became of that story, Trim?

вАФWe lost it, anвАЩ please your honour, somehow betwixt usпїњвАФbut your honour was as free from love then, as I amпїњвАФпїњвАФвАЩtwas just whilst thou wentвАЩst off with the wheelbarrowпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњwith Mrs.¬†Wadman, quoth my uncle TobyпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњShe has left a ball hereпїњвАФadded my uncle TobyпїњвАФpointing to his breastпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

вАФпїњвАФпїњShe can no more, anвАЩ please your honour, stand a siege, than she can flyпїњвАФcried the corporalпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

вАФпїњвАФпїњBut as we are neighbours, Trim,пїњвАФthe best way I think is to let her know it civilly firstпїњвАФquoth my uncle Toby.

Now if I might presume, said the corporal, to differ from your honourпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

вАФWhy else do I talk to thee, Trim? said my uncle Toby, mildlyпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

вАФThen I would begin, anвАЩ please your honour, with making a good thundering attack upon her, in returnпїњвАФand telling her civilly afterwardsпїњвАФfor if she knows anything of your honourвАЩs being in love, before handпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњLпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњd help her!пїњвАФshe knows no more at present of it, Trim, said my uncle TobyпїњвАФthan the child unbornпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФ

Precious souls!пїњвАФпїњвАФпїњвАФ

Mrs.¬†Wadman had told it, with all its circumstances, to Mrs.¬†Bridget twenty-four hours before; and was at that very moment sitting in council with her, touching some slight misgivings with regard to the issue of the affairs, which the Devil, who never lies dead in a ditch, had put into her headпїњвАФbefore he would allow half time, to get quietly through her Te Deum.

I am terribly afraid, said widow Wadman, in case I should marry him, BridgetпїњвАФthat the poor captain will not enjoy his health, with the monstrous wound upon his groinпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

It may not, Madam, be so very large, replied Bridget, as you thinkпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњand I believe, besides, added sheпїњвАФthat вАЩtis dried upпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

вАФпїњвАФпїњI could like to knowпїњвАФmerely for his sake, said Mrs.¬†WadmanпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

вАФWeвАЩll know the long and the broad of it, in ten daysпїњвАФanswered Mrs.¬†Bridget, for whilst the captain is paying his addresses to youпїњвАФIвАЩm confident Mr.¬†Trim will be for making love to meпїњвАФand IвАЩll let him as much as he willпїњвАФadded BridgetпїњвАФto get it all out of himпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

The measures were taken at onceпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњand my uncle Toby and the corporal went on with theirs.

Now, quoth the corporal, setting his left hand akimbo, and giving such a flourish with his right, as just promised successпїњвАФand no moreпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњif your honour will give me leave to lay down the plan of this attackпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

вАФпїњвАФпїњThou wilt please me by it, Trim, said my uncle Toby, exceedinglyпїњвАФand as I foresee thou must act in it as my aid de camp, hereвАЩs a crown, corporal, to begin with, to steep thy commission.

Then, anвАЩ please your honour, said the corporal (making a bow first for his commission)пїњвАФwe will begin with getting your honourвАЩs laced clothes out of the great campaign-trunk, to be well airвАЩd, and have the blue and gold taken up at the sleevesпїњвАФand IвАЩll put your white ramallie-wig fresh into pipesпїњвАФand send for a tailor, to have your honourвАЩs thin scarlet breeches turnвАЩdпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњ

вАФI had better take the red plush ones, quoth my uncle TobyпїњвАФпїњвАФпїњThey will be too clumsyпїњвАФsaid the corporal.
